elements.sent-2411-001: Universal power laws have been scrutinised in physics and beyond, and a long-standing debate exists in econophysics regarding the strict universality of the nonlinear price impact, commonly referred to as the square-root law (SRL).
elements.sent-2411-002: The SRL posits that the average price impact $I$ follows a power law with respect to transaction volume $Q$, such that $I(Q) \propto Q^{\delta}$ with $\delta \approx 1/2$.
elements.sent-2411-003: Some researchers argue that the exponent $\delta$ should be system-specific, without universality.
elements.sent-2411-004: Conversely, others contend that $\delta$ should be exactly $1/2$ for all stocks across all countries, implying universality.
elements.sent-2411-005: However, resolving this debate requires high-precision measurements of $\delta$ with errors of around $0.1$ across hundreds of stocks, which has been extremely challenging due to the scarcity of large microscopic datasets -- those that enable tracking the trading behaviour of all individual accounts.
elements.sent-2411-006: Here we conclusively support the universality hypothesis of the SRL by a complete survey of all trading accounts for all liquid stocks on the Tokyo Stock Exchange (TSE) over eight years.
elements.sent-2411-007: Using this comprehensive microscopic dataset, we show that the exponent $\delta$ is equal to $1/2$ within statistical errors at both the individual stock level and the individual trader level.
elements.sent-2411-008: Additionally, we rejected two prominent models supporting the nonuniversality hypothesis: the Gabaix-Gopikrishnan-Plerou-Stanley and the Farmer-Gerig-Lillo-Waelbroeck models.
elements.sent-2411-009: Our work provides exceptionally high-precision evidence for the universality hypothesis in social science and could prove useful in evaluating the price impact by large investors -- an important topic even among practitioners.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=165mm]{Schematic-PriceImpact.png} \caption{ Square-root price impact law. When a trader executes a large volume of order (called a metaorder, with size $Q$), they typically split them into a long sequence of child orders (with length $L$). The resulting price impact obeys the power law $\mcI(\mcQ)\propto c\mcQ^{\delta}$ with $\delta\approx 1/2$. } \label{fig:Schematic}
elements.sent-2411-010: Universality is one of the most appealing topics in physics, whereby common macroscopic laws are observed across various materials~\cite{StanleyB1971,GoldenfeldB .}
elements.sent-2411-011: For example, universal power laws are observed in critical phenomena, such as $M \propto |T - T_c|^{\delta}$, with an order parameter $M$ near the critical point $T_c$ (e.g., the magnetisation $M$ and temperature $T$ for a ferromagnetic system).
elements.sent-2411-012: Interestingly, the exponent $\delta$ is independent of the microscopic details of the system.
elements.sent-2411-013: Such a robust exponent is referred to as a {\it universal exponent to emphasise the special significance of universality, in contrast to a nonuniversal exponent that may be a fragile observable, as it could easily vary with perturbations to the system's microscopic parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-014: Inspired by such success, physicists have attempted to discover universal power laws beyond physics, including in social science.
elements.sent-2411-015: For instance, in finance, some physicists believe the {\it square-root law (SRL) for price impact~\cite{BouchaudText should be part of such universal scaling laws~\cite{Toth2011,MastromatteoPRL2014,MastromatteoPRE2014,Bucci2019PRL .}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: However, this claim of universality has not been fully established for lack of large-scale financial microscopic datasets that enable the tracking of all trading accounts individually.
elements.sent-2411-017: Here we quantitatively support the universality hypothesis through a complete survey of all trading accounts on the Tokyo Stock Exchange (TSE), aiming to conclude the long-standing debate in econophysics.
elements.sent-2411-018: The SRL is an empirical law that describes the nonlinear price response following large buying (selling) by institutional investors.
elements.sent-2411-019: Consider a trader who buys (or sells) $Q$ units of stock.
elements.sent-2411-020: Following this transaction, the market responds with a price movement $\Delta p$.
elements.sent-2411-021: For small $Q$, a linear response is expected, such that $I(Q):= \la \eps \Delta p \mid Q\ra \approx \lambda Q$, where $I(Q)$ is called the {\it price impact , $\eps$ is the order sign defined by $\eps=+1$ ($\eps=-1$) for buying (selling), and $\la A \mid B\ra$ represents the average of $A$ conditioned on $B$.}
elements.sent-2411-022: For large $Q$, on the contrary, a nonlinear response is empirically observed (see Fig.~\ref{fig:Schematic ), \begin{equation}\mcI(\mcQ) \approx c_0 \mcQ^{\delta}\label{eq:SRL-scaling}\end{equation} after the nondimensionalisation $\mcQ:=Q/V_{\Day}$ and $\mcI(\mcQ):= I(Q)/\sigma_{\Day}$, where $V_{\Day}$ is the daily transaction volume and $\sigma_{\Day}$ is the daily volatility.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Surprisingly, the exponent $\delta$ is typically claimed to be close to one-half for any asset all over the world, \begin{equation}\delta \approx \frac{1}{2}\label{eq:SRL-exponent}\end{equation} and, thus, the nonlinear scaling~\eqref{eq:SRL-scaling is referred to as the SRL~\cite{Toth2011, MastromatteoPRL2014, MastromatteoPRE2014, Bucci2019PRL,DonierLOB2015 .}}
elements.sent-2411-024: The SRL closely resembles the universal power law observed in physics and has been an exceptionally appealing topic in econophysics (see the {\SM (\SMabb) for a detailed literature review).}
elements.sent-2411-025: However, there has been a long-standing debate regarding the strict universality of the SRL.
elements.sent-2411-026: Some physicists argue that the exponent $\delta$ should depend on the microscopic details of financial markets and, therefore, $\delta$ should be considered a nonuniversal exponent.
elements.sent-2411-027: For instance, the Gabaix-Gopikrishnan-Plerou-Stanley (GGPS) and the Farmer-Gerig-Lillo-Waelbroeck (FGLW) models are two promising microscopic frameworks that support the nonuniversality hypothesis~\cite{NatureGabaix,Gabaix2006,FGLWQFin2013 .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Conversely, other physicists contend that $\delta$ should be exactly one-half for all financial markets in any country, making $\delta$ a universal exponent.
elements.sent-2411-029: For example, the latent order-book model~\cite{Toth2011,MastromatteoPRL2014,MastromatteoPRE2014,DonierLOB2015 proposes a minimal mechanism to derive $\delta=1/2$, supporting the universality hypothesis.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Which hypothesis is ultimately correct? Despite its evident importance, this debate has not yet been settled for several reasons.
elements.sent-2411-031: First, microscopic financial datasets -- those that enable tracking trading behaviour for all individual trading accounts -- are essential to test these hypotheses.
elements.sent-2411-032: Unfortunately, such microscopic datasets are extremely rare, making it challenging to rigorously test these hypotheses.
elements.sent-2411-033: Second, while previous studies have investigated the SRL, their datasets were typically proprietary, provided by specific hedge funds.
elements.sent-2411-034: This means that their datasets are neither comprehensive nor randomly sampled, which introduces the possibility of sampling bias, and the resulting sample sizes are inevitably small.
elements.sent-2411-035: A complete survey -- studying all trading accounts for all liquid stocks -- has been highly desired to address this issue convincingly.
elements.sent-2411-036: Third, solving these issues requires extremely high accuracy in measuring the exponent $\delta$.
elements.sent-2411-037: To test these hypotheses, we need to measure $\delta$ with an accuracy of about $0.1$ for hundreds of stocks.
elements.sent-2411-038: Such a high-precision measurement is difficult to achieve in social sciences for lack of large microscopic datasets.
elements.sent-2411-039: In this article, we present conclusive evidence supporting the universality of the SRL by a complete survey of the TSE.
elements.sent-2411-040: We analysed a large, high-quality microscopic dataset that records the percise trading behaviour of all trading accounts on the TSE for all stocks over eight years.
elements.sent-2411-041: Our TSE dataset includes virtual server identifiers (IDs) -- a unit of trading accounts on the TSE -- enabling us to investigate the resulting price impact $\Delta p$ associated with the buy (sell) volume $Q$ at the individual account level.
elements.sent-2411-042: Using this dataset, we precisely measured $\delta$ by controlling errors of the order of $0.07$ and found that $\delta$ was equal to $1/2$ for all liquid stocks (more than two thousand data points) on the TSE within the statistical errors.
elements.sent-2411-043: We also measured $\delta$ for all active traders individually and found it to be equal to $1/2$ within statistical errors as well.
elements.sent-2411-044: Furthermore, we rejected two prominent models that have theoretically supported the nonuniversality hypothesis: the GGPS and the FGLW models.
elements.sent-2411-045: Our result supports the universality hypothesis of the SRL with extraordinary accuracy and suggests that it can be robustly used to estimate transaction costs for institutional investors.
elements.sent-2411-046: Our dataset was provided by Japan Exchange (JPX) Group, Inc., the platform manager of the TSE market.
elements.sent-2411-047: This dataset records the complete life cycle of all order submissions for all stocks on the TSE from 4th January 2012 to 2nd November 2019.
elements.sent-2411-048: The unique feature of our dataset is that it includes virtual server IDs.
elements.sent-2411-049: Virtual server IDs are not technically equivalent to the membership IDs, because any trader may possess several virtual servers.
elements.sent-2411-050: However, we can define effective trader IDs, referred to as {\it trading desks , by tracking all the virtual server IDs (see Method, the {\SMabb , and our previous studies~\cite{Goshima2019,SatoPRL2023,SatoPRR2023 ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: In this article, we refer to these trading desks as trader IDs for short.
elements.sent-2411-052: We focus on price impacts during the continuous double auction sessions: from 09:00 to 11:30 and from 12:30 to 15:00 Japan Standard Time.
elements.sent-2411-053: The dataset was divided into two periods: from 4th January 2012 to 18th September 2015 (Dataset 1) and from 24th September 2015 to 2nd November 2019 (Dataset 2).
elements.sent-2411-054: Note that technical updates were made to the trading system (called {\it arrowhead ) between the two datasets, and the trader IDs were reallocated.}
elements.sent-2411-055: We regard any stock in Dataset 1 as a different stock from Dataset 2, even if the ticker code remains the same.
elements.sent-2411-056: For this definition, we have 942 stocks in Dataset 1 and 1,357 stocks in Dataset 2.
elements.sent-2411-057: Therefore, we can measure $\delta$ for these 2,299 datapoints.

elements.sent-2411-058: We measured the transaction volume $Q$ and corresponding price impact $\Delta p$ for all liquid stocks on the TSE.
elements.sent-2411-059: First, we defined trader IDs for each stock and extracted the market-order volume sequences.
elements.sent-2411-060: Second, we assume that institutional investors typically split their large orders (called metaorders) into small orders (called child orders), as suggested by the literature~\cite{LMF_PRE2005,SatoPRL2023,SatoPRR2023,Bershova,GeneralizedLMF .}
elements.sent-2411-061: Specifically, if two successive market orders have the same sign (plus for buy and minus for sell), we assume that both orders are child orders belonging to the same metaorder.
elements.sent-2411-062: The total volume $Q$ is then defined as the sum of all child-order volumes.
elements.sent-2411-063: Third, the price impact $\Delta p$ is defined as the price movement caused by such a metaorder.
elements.sent-2411-064: In this article, liquid stocks are defined as stocks where the total number of metaorders exceeds $10^5$.

elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=170mm]{FGLW-GGPS-Delta.png} \caption{ Quantitative test of the two theoretical models that have supported the nonuniversality hypothesis: Fig.~(a) for the GGPS prediction $\delta=\beta-1$ and Fig.~(b) for the FGLW prediction $\delta=\beta-1$. No correlation was found and, thus, both models were rejected. } \label{fig:FGLW-GGPW}
elements.sent-2411-088: While we presented direct evidence supporting the universality hypothesis, we now present direct negative evidence against two prominent models supporting the nonuniversality hypothesis: the GGPS and the FGLW models~\cite{NatureGabaix,Gabaix2006,FGLWQFin2013 .}
elements.sent-2411-089: The GGPS and FGLW models are based on microeconomic theory in the presence of order-splitters: Let us consider the case where an order-splitter has a metaorder with total volume $Q$ and splits it into $L$ child orders.
elements.sent-2411-090: Empirically, the distributions of the metaorder volume $Q$ and the number of corresponding child orders $L$ are known to obey power laws \cite{Bershova,SatoPRL2023,SatoPRR2023,LMF_PRE2005,NatureGabaix,Gabaix2006 : $P(Q)\propto Q^{-\beta-1}$ for large $Q$ and $P(L)\propto L^{-\alpha-1}$ for large $L$, where $\beta$ and $\alpha$ are positive power-law exponents characterising individual stocks.}
elements.sent-2411-091: Based on this key assumption, the GGPS and FGLW models consider the strategy optimisation of market makers in light of inventory risk and fair pricing, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-092: They predict that the exponent $\delta$ is given by \begin{equation}GGPS: \delta = \beta - 1, \>\>\> FGLW: \delta = \alpha - 1, \label{eq:prediction-nonuniversal}\end{equation} where Zipf's law is assumed for the company-size distribution as for the GGPS theory.
elements.sent-2411-093: Interestingly, the typical values of $\beta$ and $\alpha$ are distributed around 1.5, which is superficially consistent with the SRL~\cite{SatoPRL2023,SatoPRR2023,NatureGabaix,Gabaix2006,Bershova .}
elements.sent-2411-094: However, since $\beta$ and $\alpha$ can differ from 1.5 depending on the stock, the GGPS and FGLW models have suggested the theoretical nonuniversality of the exponent $\delta$.
elements.sent-2411-095: Their prediction~\eqref{eq:prediction-nonuniversal can be tested in principle if we can precisely estimate $\beta$, $\alpha$, and $\delta$ with a precision of $0.1$.}
elements.sent-2411-096: While a small group of researchers tested this relation~\eqref{eq:prediction-nonuniversal , both positive and negative results~\cite{MastromatteoPRE2014,NatureGabaix,Gabaix2006,Bershova coexist, as direct precise measurement without bias is not an easy task for $\beta$, $\alpha$, and $\delta$, except in a complete survey.}}
elements.sent-2411-097: Evidently, concluding this debate has been very challenging without large comprehensive microscopic datasets.
elements.sent-2411-098: In this article, we address this issue definitively using our large TSE dataset.
elements.sent-2411-099: To test the GGPS and FGLW models, it is sufficient to present two high-accuracy scatterplots: one between $\beta$ and $\delta$, and the other between $\alpha$ and $\delta$.
elements.sent-2411-100: We present Fig.~\ref{fig:FGLW-GGPW as our final main result.}
elements.sent-2411-101: Clearly, there is no correlation at all, rejecting both of the two prominent models that have supported the universality hypothesis, at least on the TSE.
elements.sent-2411-102: We investigated the large microscopic dataset on the Tokyo Stock Exchange over eight years and scrutinised the long-standing universality vs. nonuniversality debate regarding the square-root price impact law.
elements.sent-2411-103: We present that (i)~the square-root law holds with an exact exponent $\delta=1/2$ within statistical errors both at the stock and trader levels, and (ii)~the GGPS and FGLW models -- two prominent models theoretically supporting the nonuniversality hypothesis -- were rejected.
elements.sent-2411-104: These results provide the strongest evidence so far supporting the universality hypothesis, at least for the Tokyo Stock Exchange.
elements.sent-2411-105: We recommend that interested readers refer to the SI, where we thoroughly checked the statistical robustness of our findings.
elements.sent-2411-106: Technically, our simulation shows a small bias in our current $\delta$ estimator, such that $e_{\delta :=\dla \delta\dra - 1/2 = -0.011$, due to the finite sample-size effect.}
elements.sent-2411-107: Given this bias, one of the simplest methods for constructing unbiased estimates of $\delta$'s mean is to use $\la \delta_{\rm unbiased \ra := \la \delta \ra - e_{\delta}$ for bias removal.}
elements.sent-2411-108: Consequently, we obtain the unbiased mean \begin{equation}\la\delta_{\rm unbiased}\ra = 0.500 \pm 0.0020\end{equation} for our dataset, precisely supporting the SRL.
elements.sent-2411-109: However, our numerical null model is too simple to capture the complete price-impact dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-110: Considering the potential modelling error, there may still be room to improve the $\delta$ estimator for faster convergence with exact unbiasedness.
elements.sent-2411-111: This topic is left for future studies.
elements.sent-2411-112: Our finding implies that microscopic theories behind the square-root law should yield the universal power-law exponent $\delta = 1/2$, which does not depend on the details of the financial market microstructure, such as the latent order-book theory~\cite{BouchaudText,Toth2011,DonierLOB2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-113: It would be a future issue to directly validate or falsify such theories using our microscopic dataset.
elements.sent-2411-114: Additionally, as $\delta$ is universally $1/2$, it would be very interesting to study the system-specific details of the coefficient $c$.
elements.sent-2411-115: Regarding $c$, several natural questions arise: Is it possible to predict $c$ from microscopic financial models? For which types of stocks does $c$ tend to be larger or smaller? If there is a sudden change in the market microstructure, such as a tick-size change, does $c$ change significantly? These questions are very important not only from an academic perspective but also from the viewpoint of practitioners.
elements.sent-2411-116: Indeed, $c$ characterises the true transaction cost associated with large metaorder splitting by institutional investors.
elements.sent-2411-117: Since typical investors aim to minimise transaction costs, the microscopic mechanism behind the square-root law is an exciting topic for practitioners.
elements.sent-2411-118: While these questions are evidently important, the lack of large microscopic datasets has hindered solid data analyses regarding the coefficient $c$.
elements.sent-2411-119: Indeed, in most of the previous studies on the square-root law, transaction datasets from different stocks were mixed when calculating the average price impact, based on the strong assumption that the dimensional analysis in Eq.~\eqref{eq:SRL-scaling is perfectly correct, allowing different datasets to be mixed after non-dimensionalisation.}
elements.sent-2411-120: As a side effect, it has been very challenging to obtain the coefficient $c$ for individual stocks.
elements.sent-2411-121: In contrast, since we precisely measured the coefficient $c$ for all stocks individually in this paper, it is now possible, in principle, to investigate the stock-by-stock mechanisms that microscopically determine $c$.
elements.sent-2411-122: Thus, our work strongly motivates the opening of a new field of statistical-physics theories focusing on the coefficient $c$.
elements.sent-2411-123: Economically, the universality of the square-root law suggests that liquidity should be measured by the coefficient $c$ for large institutional investors.
elements.sent-2411-124: In traditional economics, linear-response theory $I(Q) \approx \lambda Q$ has been assumed, and the linear coefficient $\lambda$ -- known as Kyle's lambda, after Kyle's pioneering work~\cite{Kyle -- has been used as a liquidity measure.}
elements.sent-2411-125: While this metric is plausible for small transactions, the square-root law demonstrates that Kyle's lambda systematically underestimates the actual liquidity for large metaorder splitting because $Q \gg \sqrt{Q}$ for large $Q$.
elements.sent-2411-126: Thus, our study sheds new light on the quantification of market liquidity in the presence of large institutional investors, an urgent topic relevant to policy evaluation in managing exchange markets.
elements.sent-2411-127: We used the JPX order-book dataset on the TSE.
elements.sent-2411-128: This data covers the complete life cycle of all orders, from submissions to transactions/cancellations, for all stocks on the TSE over eight years (from 4th January 2012 to 2nd November 2019).
elements.sent-2411-129: We divided this dataset into two datasets: one from 4 January 2012 to 18 September 2015 (Dataset 1), and another from 24 September 2015 to 2 November 2019 (Dataset 2).
elements.sent-2411-130: This is because there was a technical update on the {\it arrowhead -- the order-matching system managed by the TSE -- on 24 September 2015, and all the virtual server IDs were shuffled.}
elements.sent-2411-131: A liquid stock is defined as one where the total number of metaorders exceeds $10^5$.
elements.sent-2411-132: This filter is introduced to control the sample size and the resulting accuracy of estimation.
elements.sent-2411-133: In addition, any stock in Dataset 1 is regarded as different from stocks in Dataset 2, even if their ticker codes are identical.
elements.sent-2411-134: With this definition, the total number of liquid stocks in Dataset 1 and Dataset 2 are 942 and 1,357, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-135: Thus, we have 2,299 datapoints in total regarding liquid stocks.
elements.sent-2411-136: Our JPX dataset records virtual server IDs corresponding to each order submission.
elements.sent-2411-137: A virtual server acts as an effective unit of trading accounts on the TSE, as all orders are submitted from a virtual server.
elements.sent-2411-138: Technically, a virtual server ID is not exactly technically equivalent to a membership ID, as any membership may possess several virtual servers.
elements.sent-2411-139: In fact, there is a limit on the total number of submissions that can be made during a fixed interval from one virtual server, and high-frequency traders might possess several virtual servers to circumvent this limit.
elements.sent-2411-140: However, we can allocate effective trader IDs by appropriate aggregation of virtual server IDs.

elements.sent-2411-141: Effective trader IDs can be defined as follows: While the lifecycle of a single order ID is typically managed by a single virtual server, there are exceptional cases where multiple virtual servers are involved in the lifecycle of one order.
elements.sent-2411-142: For example, consider the case where virtual server ``V1" submits a limit order, and then virtual server ``V2" cancels the same limit order under the same order ID ``O1" (see Table~\ref{method:tab:trading-desk ).}
elements.sent-2411-143: In this instance, we infer that the same trader operates both virtual servers, V1 and V2, and we assign a unified identifier, ``T1," as the effective trader ID.

elements.sent-2411-194: In this section, we briefly review the previous literature to provide enough background knowledge about the square-root price impact law in financial economics and econophysics.
elements.subsection-2411-009: Definition: the square-root price impact law
elements.sent-2411-195: The focus of this paper is the {\it price impact in financial markets.}
elements.sent-2411-196: In financial markets, traders can buy (sell) $Q$ unit of stocks immediately by sending a market order, and the price changes as the market's response.
elements.sent-2411-197: The average price change is called {\it the price impact , which is defined by \begin{equation}I(Q) := \left\la \eps\left( p(t_{\rm end}) - p(t_{\rm start}) \right) \mid Q\right\ra,\end{equation} where $\eps$ is the sign of the market order (such as $\eps=+1$ ($\eps=-1$) for the buy (sell) market orders) and $t_{\rm end}$ ($t_{\rm start}$) is the ending (starting) time of the market order.}
elements.sent-2411-198: Because the buy (sell) market order will increase (decrease) the market price on average, the price impact should be strictly positive $I(Q)>0$.
elements.sent-2411-199: For small perturbation, the linear-response theory is expected to work like physics.
elements.sent-2411-200: Indeed, the price impact is known to be linear for small $Q$, such that $I(Q)\propto \lambda Q$.
elements.sent-2411-201: The coefficient $\lambda$ is called Kyle's lambda after Kyle's pioneering theory~\cite{Kyle , and many economic models theoretically support the linear law of price impact.}
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=180mm]{Schematic-PriceImpact-SI.png} \caption{ Schematic of non-linear price impact. When institutional investors execute large orders (metaorders), they split them into a sequence of child orders. The metaorder splitting is characterised by two quantities: the number of child orders $L$ and the metaorder volume $Q$. These parameters are assumed to obey the power-law distributions, such that $P(L)\propto L^{-\alpha-1}, P(Q)\propto Q^{-\beta-1}$. } \label{pic:squarerootlaw}
elements.sent-2411-202: For large perturbation, however, such a linear-response theory is experimentally known to be incorrect.
elements.sent-2411-203: Instead, the power-law price impact is observed as the market's nonlinear response, such that \begin{equation}I(Q)\propto c\sigma_{\Day} \left( \frac{Q}{V_{\Day}}\right)^{\delta}\>\> for large $Q$, \label{eq:lreview-SQL-def}\end{equation} where $\delta$ is the characteristic power-law exponent, $V_{\Day}$ is the daily transaction volume, $\sigma_{\Day}$ is the daily volatility, and $c$ is a non-dimensional constant of $O(1)$ (see Fig.~\ref{pic:squarerootlaw as a schematic).}
elements.sent-2411-204: This nonlinear price impact law is called the {\it square-root law because the typical value of $\delta$ is one half: \begin{equation}\delta \approx \frac{1}{2}. \label{eq:lreview-delta=1/2}\end{equation} In this paper, $\delta$ is the central quantity and is presicely measured for solving a long-standing problem in econophysics.}
elements.sent-2411-205: We stress that the nonlinear-response relation~\eqref{eq:lreview-SQL-def is practically important.}
elements.sent-2411-206: Indeed, institutional investors (such as hedge funds) often buy (sell) large amounts of stocks when they believe the stock price will go up (down) in future.
elements.sent-2411-207: They typically split the large {\it metaorder into a sequence of {\it child orders .}}
elements.sent-2411-208: This strategic behaviour is called {\it order splitting and has been a central topic in econophysics~\cite{BouchaudText,SatoPRL2023,SatoPRR2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-209: Institutional investors practically use the formula~\eqref{eq:lreview-SQL-def to evaluate the transaction cost during order splitting.}
elements.subsection-2411-010: Previous reports on the values of $\delta$
elements.sent-2411-210: It is not easy to measure $\delta$ because it requires microscopic financial datasets to track traders' behaviour at an individual level.
elements.sent-2411-211: Most of public datasets do not include traders' identifiers; the metaorder size $Q$ and the correspoinding price impact $I(Q)$ cannot be estimated in principle.
elements.sent-2411-212: Due to the scarcity of microscopic datasets, there has been no reliable complete survey of $\delta$ for all the stocks and all the traders.
elements.sent-2411-213: While there has been no complete survey, a small group of researchers measured $\delta$ using their proprietry datasets.
elements.sent-2411-214: Here the proprietry datasets mean that they use trading-log datasets of some specific institutions.
elements.sent-2411-215: For example, the Paris group of Bouchaud is very famous because they manage their own hedge fund (Capital Fund Management) and experimentally measure $\delta$ by their own trading records.
elements.sent-2411-216: Table~\ref{tab:MetaOrder summarises the previous values of $\delta$ based on such proprietry datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-217: The exponent $\delta$ typically distributes around $0.5$, and the nonlinear-response relation~\eqref{eq:lreview-SQL-def is thus called the square-root law.}
elements.subsection-2411-011: Long-standing problem: universal or nonuniversal power-law exponent?
elements.sent-2411-218: We next formulate the research topic in this article.
elements.sent-2411-219: First, we defined the {\it universal (nonuniversal) power-law exponent as a technical word in traditional statistical physics.}
elements.sent-2411-220: We next proceed with the ongoing debate in market microstructure/econophysics regarding the universality argument of the square-root law.
elements.sent-2411-221: We then discuss the reason why this problem has not been solved for a long year from the viewpoint of the difficulty in statistical analysis and data availability.
elements.sent-2411-222: Finally, we clarify the goal of this article together with our solution.
elements.subsubsection-2411-001: Review: universal power-law exponents in physics
elements.sent-2411-223: In statistical physics, the power-law behaviour is ubiquitously observed, particularly in the context of phase transition and critical phenomena.
elements.sent-2411-224: For example, the ferromagnetic material exhibits the second-order phase transition, and the magnetization $m$ shows the power-law behaviour \begin{equation}m \sim \begin{cases} |T-T_c|^{\delta} (T\leq T_c) 0 (T>T_c) \end{cases}\end{equation} near the critical temperature $T_c>0$.
elements.sent-2411-225: Here the power-law exponent $\delta$ characterises the phase transition and is known to be determined only by the symmetry and dimension of the target system.
elements.sent-2411-226: For example, let us consider the Ising model as the minimal model of the ferromagnetic material.
elements.sent-2411-227: Beyond the upper critical dimension $D\geq 4$, the Landau mean-field theory predicts $\delta = 1/2$, which is exactly correct.
elements.sent-2411-228: For $D=3$, on the other hand, the renormalization-group theory predicts the correct value of $\delta$ that depends on the system's symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-229: Such a power-law exponent is called the {\it universal power-law exponent because it is insensitive to the detail of the microscopic systems; it is not a function of microscopic parameters (such as the lattice constant and coupling constant) but is a function of only global characters of the system (such as the system's symmetry and dimension).}
elements.sent-2411-230: The universal power-law exponent is a very good quantity to measure because its value is theoretically very robust; if we want to test some microscopic theory, we should measure this exponent and compare with the theoretical prediction in validating/rejecting the theory.
elements.sent-2411-231: On the other hand, if the power-law exponent depends on the details of microscopic parameters of the target systems, the exponent is called the {\it nonuniversal power-law exponent ; it is a function of microscopic parameters $\bm{\theta :=(\theta_1,\dots, \theta_K)$, such that $\delta = \delta(\bm{\theta )$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-232: The nonuniversal power-law exponent is a fragile quantity to predict because it is sensitive to the details of the microscopic systems.
elements.sent-2411-233: If an exponent $\delta$ is nonuniversal, it means that there exist key microscopic parameters in fixing the value of $\delta$, and precise measurement of such microscopic parameters is inevitable.
elements.sent-2411-234: Let us go back to our research topic of this paper particularly for market microstructure and econophysics.
elements.sent-2411-235: Our research question is given as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-236: Is $\delta$ in the square-root law~\eqref{eq:lreview-delta=1/2 a universal power-law exponent? In other words, is $\delta$ always one-half within statistical errors, independent of the details of financial market microstructure, such as stocks and trading strategies?.}
elements.sent-2411-237: To answer this research question, we test two mutually-exclusive hypotheses as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-238: By measuring $\bm{\theta}$ from microscopic datasets, the correlation should be clearly observed between $\delta$ and $\bm{\theta}$.
elements.sent-2411-239: Finally, we rejected $H_1$ for most of the main scenarios of previous researches, but did not reject $H_0$ in our statistical analyses.
elements.sent-2411-240: We thus present the current best evidence supporting the universality argument of $\delta$.
elements.subsubsection-2411-003: Review: current theoretical arguments about the square-root law from the universality viewpoint
elements.sent-2411-241: In the literature, there are several microscopic models to explain the origin of the square-root law, and it is a central issue if $\delta$ is a universal exponent or not.
elements.sent-2411-242: For example, the following two theoretical models are famous arguing the nonuniversality of $\delta$: Gabaix-Gopikrishnan-Plerou-Stanley (GGPS) model and Farmer-Gerig-Lillo-Waelbroeck (FGLW) model.
elements.sent-2411-243: Both models share the assumptions that (i)~there are the institutional investors splitting large metaorders into sequences of child orders and (ii)~the statistics of the metaorder volume or their run length determines the exponent $\delta$.
elements.sent-2411-244: More precise formulation is given as follows: let us denote the total metaorder volume for one splitting by $Q$ and the total number of child orders (or the run length of one metaorder) by $L$.
elements.sent-2411-245: The distribution of $Q$ and $L$ are assumed to obey the power law: \begin{equation}P(Q) \propto Q^{-\beta-1}, \>\>\> P(L) \propto L^{-\alpha-1}\end{equation} for large $Q$ and $L$.
elements.sent-2411-246: GGPS and FGLW claim that the key microscopic parameter $\theta$ characterising $\delta(\theta)$ is $\beta$ and $\alpha$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-247: Because order splitters sometimes buy/sell a huge amount of stocks finally, the inventory risk is high in this setup.
elements.sent-2411-248: They therefore assume that the market maker aims control the inventory risk during their market-making (which is implemented in the utility function as the risk aversion).
elements.sent-2411-249: Finally, they claim that $\delta$ is nonuniversal; $\beta$ is the key microscopic parameter characterising $\delta$ as \begin{equation}\delta = \delta(\beta) = \beta - 1, \>\>\>\end{equation} where they claim that typical value of $\beta$ is $1.5$, assuming that the fund's asset size distribution follows Zipf's law\footnote{More technically, the exponent $\delta$ is predicted to follow $\delta=\zeta^{-1}\beta-1$ for a general $\zeta$.}
elements.sent-2411-250: This relationship clearly argues the nonuniversality of $\delta$ because it predicts that $\delta$ can be different from one-half if $\beta$ is not equal to $1.5$.
elements.sent-2411-251: Market makers attempt to set fair prices inferring the total number of metaorder length from historical order-flow data.
elements.sent-2411-252: As the Nash equilibrium, the price impact is given by a nonlinear profile with the power-law exponet \begin{equation}\delta = \delta(\alpha) = \alpha - 1,\end{equation} where the typical value of $\alpha$ is claimed to be $1.5$.
elements.sent-2411-253: This theory predicts that the key microscopic parameter is $\alpha$, and $\delta$ should deviate from one-half if $\alpha \neq 1.5$.
elements.sent-2411-254: The GGPS and FGLW models are the main microscopic models claiminig the nonuniversality of the power-law exponent $\delta$.
elements.sent-2411-255: On the other hand, there are researchers claiming the universality of the power-law exponent $\delta$.
elements.sent-2411-256: While there is no established microscopic theory to explain the origin of this universal power-law exponent, recently, the latent-order book model was proposed by Donier et al.~\cite{LatentOrderBook as a promising microscopic minimal model to understand the universality of $\delta =1/2$.}
elements.subsubsection-2411-004: Review: exising data-analytical results and their technical challenges
elements.sent-2411-257: The universal and nonuniversal hypotheses are contradictory to each other, and only one hypothesis should be correct in reality.
elements.sent-2411-258: However, the current situation is controversial from the viewpoint of data analysis.
elements.sent-2411-259: To test these hypotheses, it is necessary to measure $\delta$ for all the stocks and all the traders individually and to draw the scatterplot between $\delta$ and $\alpha$ (or $\beta$).
elements.sent-2411-260: However, there are several technical challenges, which we will explain one by one.
elements.sent-2411-261: Since such microscopic datasets are rarely available in reality, complete surveys to measure $\delta$ have been lacking so far.
elements.sent-2411-262: The typical design of previous researches has been to use proprietry datasets of some specific companies/institutions (for example, Refs.~\cite{Almgren, Zarinelli2015, Bucci2019a, BucciCoImpact2020, Bucci2020, Toth2011, Bacry2015, Bershova, MastromatteoPRL2014, MastromatteoPRE2014, Brokmann2015, TothOption, Said2021 ).}
elements.sent-2411-263: However, relying on proprietary datasets means that the statistical results might be biased because it is based neither on a complete survey nor on random sampling.
elements.sent-2411-264: To establish the universality of $\delta$, we should show that the $\delta$ depends neither on the selection of specific traders nor stocks.
elements.sent-2411-265: However, $\delta$, $\alpha$, and $\beta$ are power-law exponents, whose precise measurement requires large sample size for each stock.
elements.sent-2411-266: Also, we have to measure $(\delta, \alpha, \beta)$ for many independent stocks to draw their scatterplot to test the prediction of the GGPS and FGLW models.
elements.sent-2411-267: To provide convincing evidence, we should control the measurement errors of $(\delta, \alpha, \beta)$ within 0.1 for more than 1,000 independent stocks.
elements.sent-2411-268: They implicitly assume that the heterogeneity in market conditions is completely removed through appropriate non-dimensionalisation.
elements.sent-2411-269: For example, let us express the price impact for stock $s$ as $I_s(Q) = C_sQ^{1/2}$ and assume that the coefficient $C_s$ is always non-dimensionalised by $C_s=c_{s}\sigma_{\Day;s /V_{\Day;s ^{1/2}$, where $c_s$ is non-dimensional .}}
elements.sent-2411-270: If this scaling assumption holds completely, we would expect the average {\it across different stocks for $\mcI(\mcQ):= I_s(Q)/\sigma_{\Day;s}$ -- the dimensionless price impact conditional on the dimensionless volume $\mcQ:= Q/V_{\Day;s}$ -- to obey the square-root law.}
elements.sent-2411-271: However, this is a strong assumption that requires validation, as mixing different datasets with evident heterogeneity is not desirable in statistics.
elements.sent-2411-272: Solving all these problems is challenging, and previous research has not yet conclusively addressed our research question.
elements.sent-2411-273: For example, Bershova and Rakhlin~\cite{Bershova state that the FGLW prediction is consistent with their empirical observations using a proprietry dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-274: On the other hand, Mastromatteo, Toth, and Bouchaud~\cite{MastromatteoPRE2014 shows a scatterplot between $\delta$ and $\gamma$ -- which is defined as the power-law exponent in the market-order sign autocorrelation function and a indirect proxy of $\alpha$ because the Lillo-Mike-Farmer model~\cite{LMF_PRE2005,GeneralizedLMF states the relation $\gamma=\alpha-1$ should hold -- using their proprietry dataset.}}
elements.sent-2411-275: The total number of data points in their scatterplot is ten, which is few, and their estimator is expected to be statistically biased\footnote{ In our previous publications~\cite{SatoPRL2023,SatoPRR2023 , we found that estimating $\alpha$ is actually feasible from $\gamma$ via the Lillo-Mike-Farmer prediction $\alpha=\gamma+1$, which partially validates the statistical analysis in Ref.~\cite{MastromatteoPRE2014 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-276: However, at the same time, we found that the estimated values of $\alpha$ are shown to be systematically biased along with large statistical errors.
elements.sent-2411-277: Because the standard linear regression formulation assumes that the explanatory variables are measured without statistical errors or biases, statistical interpretation becomes more challenging than usual~\cite{SelectionBias .}
elements.sent-2411-278: Indeed, apparent correlation in scatterplots is known to be generally biased toward zero if its explanatory variable has large measurement error.
elements.sent-2411-279: This phenomenon is called the {\it attenuaion bias in statistics~\cite{SelectionBias , which might not be negligible when one uses $\gamma$ as a proxy of $\alpha$.. .}}
elements.sent-2411-280: However, their scatterplot contradicts the FGLW prediction in the sense that the correlation is not clearly observed between $\delta$ and $\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-281: In addition, most of all the previous researches are based on propriety datasets and thus selection biases are not well controlled.
elements.sent-2411-282: Thus, convincing and conclusive evidence is necessary to resolve this controversial situation.
elements.sent-2411-283: This paper aims to solve all the problems P1-P4 and to provide convincing and conclusive evidence for the universality of the square-root law.
elements.sent-2411-284: We used a microscopic dataset in the Tokyo Stock Exchange (TSE) market provided by Japan Exchange Group Inc.
elements.sent-2411-285: This dataset covers all the stocks on the TSE at the level of all individual accounts for eight years and thus enables us to track the price impact of each metaorder.
elements.sent-2411-286: The total number of stocks was about one thousand, and the total number of metaorders across all the stocks was approximately $10^8$, which is sufficient as the data size in controlling the measurement errors of $\delta$ within 0.1 for all liquid stocks.
elements.sent-2411-287: Furthermore, we measured the price impact at both market and individual-trader levels as a complete survey, for which we can completely control sampling bias.
elements.sent-2411-288: We thus design our statistical analysis as a three steps:.
elements.sent-2411-289: Result 1) We measured $\delta$ for {\it all stocks individually to plot $\delta$'s histogram, supporting the universality hypothesis $\delta=0.5$ on the stock level.}
elements.sent-2411-290: Result 2) We measured $\delta$ for {\it all traders individually to plot $\delta$'s histogram, supporting the universality hypothesis $\delta=0.5$ on the trader level.}
elements.sent-2411-291: Result 3) We rejected the main predictions of the GGPS and FGLW models, which were previously considered promising in supporting the nonuniversal-exponent hypothesis.
elements.sent-2411-292: In this research, we use trade and quote data provided by JPX Group Inc., which manages the platform for the TSE.
elements.sent-2411-293: Our dataset contains complete records of order submissions for all the listed stocks on the TSE; i.e., all traders' order submission activities are trackable.
elements.sent-2411-294: In this report, we analysed all liquid stocks, exchange traded funds (ETFs), and real estate investment trusts (REITs) listed on the TSE market from 4 January 2012 to 2 November 2019.
elements.sent-2411-295: One of the significant advantages of our dataset is that it contains virtual server IDs, which enable us to track metaorder execution.
elements.sent-2411-296: The virtual server identifier (ID) is a unit of account in the TSE.
elements.sent-2411-297: Although it is not completely equivalent to the membership ID, appropriate aggregation of virtual server IDs, {\it the trading desks ~\cite{Goshima2019 , can be an effective proxy of the membership ID.}}
elements.sent-2411-298: In this report, the trading desk is regarded as the effective membership ID and is called the trader ID for short (for details of the trading desk, see Appendix~\ref{app:sec:tradingdesk and Refs.~\cite{SatoPRL2023, SatoPRR2023 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-299: In this section, we explain the trading rules of the TSE.
elements.sent-2411-300: Understanding these rules is crucial as our data preprocessing is based on them.
elements.sent-2411-301: By briefly describing the trading rules, we aim to provide a clear basis for our data preprocessing approach.
elements.sent-2411-302: Let us describe the trading system at the TSE.
elements.sent-2411-303: The TSE employs an order-matching engine known as {\it arrowhead , whose performance significantly influences the matching speed.}
elements.sent-2411-304: The arrowhead system was updated on 24 September 2015, improving its reaction speed to ten times faster than before.
elements.sent-2411-305: Such a technical update may influence trading behaviour, for example, among high-frequency traders (HFTs) or algorithmic traders.
elements.sent-2411-306: It could also impact our statistical analysis.
elements.sent-2411-307: We have, therefore, divided the dataset into two periods: one from 4 January 2012 to 18 September 2015, and another from 24 September 2015 to 2 November 2019.
elements.sent-2411-308: We refer to the former as Dataset 1 and the latter as Dataset 2.
elements.sent-2411-309: In the TSE, there are three types of trading sessions (see Fig.~\ref{fig:Preprocessing for a schematic): (i) the opening auction during 08:00-09:00 and 12:05-12:30, (ii) the continuous-double auction during 09:00-11:30 and 12:30-15:00, and (iii) the closing auction at 11:30 and 15:00.}
elements.sent-2411-310: Note that the time zone is set to be Japan; Japan Standard Time (JST) is equivalent to UTC+9.
elements.sent-2411-311: During the opening auction, orders are collected but not immediately executed; they are executed only at the end of the opening auctions, either at 09:00 or 12:30.
elements.sent-2411-312: Conversely, during the continuous double auctions, all orders can be immediately executed through market-order submissions.
elements.sent-2411-313: This paper focuses on studying the order flow during the continuous double auction periods, particularly the price impact of metaorders.
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=170mm]{JPX_TradingPeriod_OurAnalysis-v2.png} \caption{ The trading sessions at the TSE include three types: (i) the opening auction from 08:00 to 09:00 and from 12:05 to 12:30, (ii) the continuous double auction from 09:00 to 11:30 and from 12:30 to 15:00, and (iii) the closing auction at 11:30 and at 15:00. } \label{fig:Preprocessing}

elements.section-2411-013: Statistical properties of our statistical methods in finite sample size
elements.sent-2411-456: Particularly, we study two issues: (i) {\it unbiasedness of our estimators and (ii) the standard deviation of $\delta_s$ and $\chi_s$ due to finite sample-size effect.}
elements.sent-2411-457: Unbiasedness is one of the main desirable characters of estimators: for an estimator $\hat{\theta}$ with a true value $\theta$ and a sample size $N$, the estimator is called unbiased if its average is equal to the true value even for a finite sample size: \begin{equation}\la \hat{\theta}\ra = \theta for N<\infty.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-458: While it is feasible to construct unbiased estimators for simple setups (e.g., independently and identically distributed (IID) random variables), constructing exactly unbiased estimators for more complex setups, such as high-dimensional time series models, is challenging.
elements.sent-2411-459: In our case, the price impacts of metaorders are not independent due to the presence of several metaorder splitters acting simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-460: Therefore, we numerically evaluate whether our estimator approximately satisfies unbiasedness by assuming a generative stochastic process for the price impacts.
elements.sent-2411-461: Also, we are interested in the standard deviation of the power-law exponents of $\delta_s$ due to the finite sample size, such that \begin{equation}\sigma_{\delta} := \sqrt{\la (\delta_s-\la\delta_s\ra)^2\ra}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-462: Specifically, we consider the case where the true value of $\delta_s$ is exactly $1/2$ to determine whether the empirical standard deviation in our dataset can be attributed solely to the finite sample-size effect.
elements.sent-2411-463: Let us describe the generative stochastic model of the price impact with an exact half exponent $\delta = 1/2$, based on our real dataset.
elements.sent-2411-464: The idea of our model is straightforward: (i)~All metaorder execution schedules (in physical time) are identical to those in our real dataset.
elements.sent-2411-465: The randomness in our model arises from the random shuffling in process~(ii) and the Gaussian random variables in process~(iii).
elements.sent-2411-466: Since our model exhibits the square-root law price impact with an exact half exponent $\delta = 1/2$, it is feasible to evaluate the estimation error of $\delta$ from our real data attributable to the finite sample-size effect, by assuming the exact square-root law.
elements.sent-2411-467: In the following, we fix a stock $s$ and an operating day $j$, but omit the stock symbol $s$ and the operating day $j$ from the subscripts for simplicity.
elements.sent-2411-468: Let $t$ be the tick time, which increments by one with each metaorder submission.
elements.sent-2411-469: For a tick time $t$, the corresponding physical time is denoted by $\mathfrak{t (t)$ [sec], which is identical to that in our real dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-470: At the tick time $t$, we assume that trader $i$ submits his/her $k$-th child order belonging to the $r$-th metaorder.
elements.sent-2411-471: By the metaorder submission at the tick tim $t$, the market midprice after the transaction $m(t)$ obeys \begin{equation}m(t) = m(t-1) + \Delta I_{r;k}^{(i)} + \sigma(t) \Delta W(t), \>\>\> \sigma^2(t) := \frac{\mathfrak{t}(t)-\mathfrak{t}(t-1)}{18,000 [sec]},\end{equation} where the price impact is designed to follow the exact square-root law on average, such that \begin{equation}\Delta I_{r;k}^{(i)} = c\sigma_{\Day}\eps_{r;k}^{(i)}\left\{\left(q_{r;k}^{(i)}\right)^{1/2}-\left(q_{r;k-1}^{(i)}\right)^{1/2}\right\} for $k\geq 1$, \>\>\> q_{r;0}^{(i)} := 0,\end{equation} where $q_{r;k ^{(i)}$ is the cumulative volume up to the $k$-th child order belonging to the $r$-th metaorder for trader $i$ (i.e., $0=q_{r;0 ^{(i) <q_{r;1 ^{(i) < \dots \leq Q_{r ^{(i)}$, see Fig.~\ref{fig:TotalImpact-PathImpact-Schematic for a schematic), and $\Delta W(t)$ is the standard white Gaussian noise term.}}}}}}}
